title: Today's Tech Brief: Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice
summary: A look at the intersection of AI and spirituality, plus some concerns about AI in healthcare
imageAlt: Image from source article for Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice
imageCaption: Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice
imageSourceTitle: Deus ex machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. I've got a couple of stories that caught my eye recently, and I think you'll find them interesting. First up, it seems that half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice. Now, I'm not sure if that's a comforting thought or a concerning one, but it's definitely a sign of the times we're living in.

According to a report from The Register, half of US Christians are open to receiving spiritual guidance from AI. This raises all sorts of questions about the role of technology in our spiritual lives. I mean, can a machine really provide meaningful guidance, or is it just a novelty? It's a bit like asking a GPS for directions to enlightenment – it might give you a route, but will it get you there?

In other news, Ontario auditors have found that doctors' AI note-takers are making some pretty basic mistakes. Apparently, 60% of the AI scribe systems they evaluated got patient medication notes wrong. That's not exactly the kind of accuracy you'd want when it comes to your medical records. I guess that's what happens when you rely on machines to do our work for us – sometimes they get it wrong, and sometimes they get it really wrong.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
